speaker: interviewer
text: It's March 30, 1995 and this interview is with Esther Rudomin Hautzig. Can you tell me what the eating arrangements were in Siberia for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, eating arrangements sounds much more almost elegant and precise than our arrangements actually were. We got ration cards for bread. And we got sometimes oil, cooking oil. And then we got a piece of land outside of the village where we could plant potatoes. And that was the staple of our diet-- potatoes, bread, oil, onions. And my mother would make a stew out of onions and potatoes. And she called it [? tekla, ?] which was her name for goulash. It wasn't really goulash. To get additional food, we had to sell what we brought from Vilna. So I would-- my mother didn't care to do it. But my grandmother and I liked going to the baracholka. The baracholka was like the flea market. Baracholk is spelled [? B-A-R-A-C-H-O-L-K, ?] baracholka. It was like a flea market. So we used to go and sell a slip or an umbrella or whatever we had grabbed in Vilna for either money or in exchange for food stamps on the baracholka. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was always hungry, always hungry. And that's what I remember about Siberia, is being cold and hungry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always craved sweets. And I never felt I had enough bread. I never even felt I had enough potatoes. To this day, I love potatoes. I'll make a big pot of mashed potatoes for dinner and eat it for breakfast the next morning, just because they are there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the arrangements were such as, as I described. Every week or every day was, was a struggle to have enough. Potatoes would rot. They would freeze if you didn't have a proper cellar. And frozen potatoes are one of the most evil-tasting things in the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you make do. That's all. It's not-- you get-- you know, people ask me, children ask me, how could you live like that? And one little girl wrote me a letter after a, a, a class visit I had in Great Neck. And she said, 'I learned that courage is not just doing a heroic deed, but to act without thinking while doing a frightening task,' a sixth grader.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, courage is, is not a heroic thing. You just do what you have to do without thinking. Don't have potatoes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you go to bed hungry. It's not the end of the world. You still get up in the morning. It's not like you're starving to death. But we just never had enough food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it three meals a day of potatoes and bread and onions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, bread was the, the staple of the three meals a day. Occasionally, we had a piece of meat, if we sold something. And then I started doing things to earn some money or some food. You know, it was bartering. And one day, I was on, on the baracholka. And before that, I knitted a sweater for a woman who was born with one arm. And she had a little girl. And she knew I could knit. I was taught knitting and embroidery and everything in Vilna, both in my school and by my mother and by Miss Rachel, my governess. And I knitted a sweater for this woman with one arm for her daughter. And she paid me with food-- potatoes and bread. So that gave me courage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, I met a woman named Maria Nikolaivna. Her husband was a big shot in the village. And we talked in the baracholka. And she said, would I be willing to knit a sweater for her? I wrote about that. And the seems to be the center somehow of our life in Siberia. I learned to cope by doing something myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she asked me whether I would rip apart this skit. It was a machine-made woolen skirt. And I unraveled it and tied the pieces of wool together and knitted a sweater for her. I measured her very carefully. And she was going to pay me with milk, because she got a cow. And I competed this sweater. And she came to the house-- or hut. By then, we had a hut all to ourselves, my mother, my grandmother, my father, and I. And she put on the sweater. And it didn't fit her. And she couldn't close it. And it turned out that she gained weight because she got a cow, which would give her milk with which she would pay me for the sweater. And I had to reknit the sweater. But I didn't give up. I unraveled the sweater. I made it all over again-- bigger, because she got heavier. And I got milk. And that was like a watershed. I could do something to, to earn milk. And after that, I did all sorts of other little embroidery and crocheting and knitting work for, for people in the village. And I got paid with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a dressmaker who knew this Maria Nikolaivna. And she asked me to do crocheting around collars. You know, in Europe, you had collars that went into blouses. And they were sort of like pinned down. And I crocheted little borders around them. And I went to deliver once a job that she gave me, because she was going to pay me. And my mother said I cannot go. There's going to be a terrible buran. A buran is a terrible storm. And of course, I wouldn't listen. I said, I'll be back before the buran got here. And she would-- she let me. She let me go. And the buran actually came before I came home. I was in the middle of the road coming back to our hut. And I was lost. I didn't know which way to go. And suddenly, I heard a voice. And I thought that I was hallucinating. But it was my mother's voice and she was calling me-- my name. And she was calling out the shamah. A woman who wasn't a religious person was calling the shamah. And she guided me. And one of the covers of this book in, in a foreign language, in Danish, has a picture of, of the mother and the child meeting each other in the storm. Storms killed as many people as hunger. Because if there is a terrible buran and you're lost and you sit down out of fatigue or malnutrition, you freeze to death. Of course, we had typhoid and, and all sorts of other infectious diseases. My grandfather died in, in, in this labor camp, chopping down trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you, when you left your home in Vilna, you didn't really get to grab anything. You tried to grab the photo albums, but your mother wouldn't let you. Living in Siberia, in the freezing cold, what clothing did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had one pair of shoes that I had. And my mother didn't pack another pair of shoes. But she packed a winter coat. I had a winter coat. I had one sweater. And I had one dress. Of course, you grow. So you just sort of, you sell something or you trade something that you have for something else. My mother packed the most outlandish things. She took silk slips. And she took silver spoons. Someone said-- it was in people grabbed anything when they were-- and it's true. When I saw the movie, I realized that my mother grabbed things. She didn't pack sensibly.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't have shoes after a year. So in the, in the warm weather, I walked around barefoot. Because where we were in Rubtsovsk, R-U-B-T-S-O-V-S-K, Rubtsovsk, which is near Barnaul, B-A-R-N-A-U-L, which is the larger city in that area, we wore something called [NON-ENGLISH]. They are woolen boots made out of pressed wool. And sometimes, I had to wear them when the snow melted. So it was like walking with these heavy, wet boots. I couldn't wait for it to get warm enough, even not warm enough, not, not so cold that I couldn't walk around barefoot.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had that coat until it was like a jacket. And finally, I bought a fufaika, which is like a quilted jacket. I made enough money to buy one. I didn't have to trade. And I thought I was just-- o, I was the best-dressed person in the world, because I had a, a fufaika that fitted.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the school situation in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school situation was that we got a very good education there. We had many excellent teachers, because don't forget the Second World War didn't just affect Poland and the three Baltic countries. The Soviet Union was occupied almost to Moscow. And people were running out of Central Russia from Byelorussia, from the Ukraine, from the Moscow and Leningrad areas, to Siberia. And we-- I was taught Russian literature by a former university professor of, of Russian literature. Anna Simnionimya was her name. A-N-N-A Simnionimya-- this is patronymic. Her father's name was Simnion. And I called her Anna Simnionimya. But I do not know her last name. She was the most wonderful teacher. She gave me such encouragement in writing. Because of her, I always felt, well, I could tell a story on paper if I had paper, which was not that often. We used newsprint to do homework on and to write on and pages out of books which were incomplete. We wrote on that. And I studied math, which I always failed, and algebra, which I always flunked. But my worst problem was to make friends in the beginning. I didn't know any Russian. And I was an enemy of the people. And I came from Poland. And the few things I had were pretty, even though they were too small. And my quote, unquote, 'friends,' classmates, were jealous of me. I had nice, long hair, braids. And all I wanted to do is to have a friend. And when you have a friend and you have people who love you, a family, you can take almost anything, except [NON-ENGLISH] gas ovens. And I finally made a friend. Here name was Svetlana. I cut my hair. I no longer looked as pretty, I guess. I still wore the same sweater. And she, she had more than I. Because the second winter in school, I still wore the same clothes I wore the first time. But once you have a friend, you have more than one friend. And it doesn't even matter if you have more than one.  I had a Russian friend. And that just made life much more bearable. A kid is a kid. A 12-year-old is 12-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you become friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: By virtue of proximity and my never sort of saying, OK, I give up. I never give up. I never gave up. That, I learned from my mother. In her quiet way, she went-- took one step at a time. And I learned from her by example, more than by teaching. You wanted to have friends. You kind of-- you were nice to other people, until finally, they thought, well, maybe she's not so bad. And she cut her hair. And she doesn't look so great. And she still wears the same sweater.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know that things don't change? Children in schools in America who have to change schools have the same problems. Just don't give up. I spoke at a school which prepares youngsters in New York for so-called better schools than those in their areas. And one boy came up to me. And he said, tell me, did you ever want to give up? Did you ever feel like committing suicide?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, never. Don't you ever forget, you go to bed. And the next morning is a new day, just one after another. I knew what he meant. To them, reading was like facing their own Siberias. Everybody has their Siberias. You don't give up. That's how I made friends with Svetlana. And that made it just so much easier. It wasn't an easy-- it was a very difficult life for five and a half years. But it wasn't life-threatening every day, like the, the SS, or being in a concentration camp, or being in a ghetto and not knowing whether someone isn't going to shoot you down on the street. People weren't shooting us on the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it never came into question. That's why the woman in that school who said, how can you say you're a Holocaust survivor? Where is your number? Well, my number isn't here. I have something still here from those years. And that doesn't go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm always driving everybody crazy to eat. And it's not-- it's not funny. And it makes them crazy. It wasn't easy for my children, especially my daughter. She was an only child for the first eight and a half years. Her, her brother, my, our son, was born eight and a half years later. And one day, she pushed away a perfectly nice plate of food. I don't like that. I said, Debbie, don't talk like that. I was really angry. She was about six and a half. She was born in 1956, so that was in about '62, 20 years after I left Siberia, or lived in Siberia,

speaker: interviewee
text: And she couldn't make it to her room. She collapsed in the chair in the living room and cried and cried and cried. And I said, Deborah, what's wrong? She said, Ma, it wasn't my fault that you were hungry when you were a little kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: Holocaust survivors' children have a lot of things to contend with. The war doesn't just affect one generation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It affects future generations, too. I saw it in my house. My son didn't even read any-- he didn't want to hear about Siberia. He was smart in some ways. He protected himself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I once was watching a Holocaust program and crying. And he said, why are you watching it? I said, because I owe it to Musik. And I owe it to my family. He said, well, you may owe it to them, but you don't owe it to yourself to get sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I've thought about it a lot. What do we owe? We owe a lot. And it if makes us sick to watch it, then so be it, so we are sick. But we're alive. Did you ever think that you wouldn't live--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the cold and through the disease? Were there diseases [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, typhus, and, and-- oh, I was sick so much in Siberia. I had-- I didn't know what it was, but it seems to have been rheumatic fever. And there were no antibiotics. I had constant cold, no-- colds is not it. I had pneumonia. But I never thought I would die. I just didn't want to be sick. I just-- I don't know. Maybe I was a fool, but I always-- I must be like my father in some ways. No, I never thought I would die. I was afraid my father-- my father was drafted again. He went into the army in '42. He was again a foot soldier. And I thought then that the world was coming to the end, because it was just my grandmother, my mother, and me, and nobody else to protect us. But we made friends who helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you studied in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I remember studying Pushkin and Lermontov, the great Russian poets. We studied-- we had grammar. We had literature. We had a foreign language. And in our case, it was German, because we were at-- we, in Siberia, our Soviet government, was at war with Germany. Therefore, it was important to know the enemy's language. There was physics. I was-- I came. I was 10 and 1/2. I left when I was closer to 16 than to 15. In March, '46, we left. Physics, algebra, geometry-- I didn't flunk geometry, somehow could reason-- very rigorous education. I came to America without a word of English in May, '47. And I came out of Russia in March, '46. And I made my high school in three, three semesters of high school in two. I knew a lot, although I had hardly any schooling between '46, March '46, and May, '47. When Sputnik went up, that was the space program-- in 1956, that was, I wasn't-- '57. '57. In the fall of '57, Sputnik went up. And, and I said to my American friends-- by then I was-- '57, I was in this country already 10 years. I said, I'm not surprised. I don't know why you're all surprised. In Siberia in a small village in the middle of nowhere, we had a rigorous education without paper, without notebooks, without many texts. We had to share texts. We'd pass them around. But oh, my, we were expected to really work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you nervous in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved school. I just loved school, even if I failed algebra. It was a place to be with, with, with your peers and, and not worry whether the potatoes are rotting It was, it was wonderful. I loved school. I still like going for courses and working with children in schools now. When I say my-- I know they say, your face lights up. I said, yeah, I really love school. It just sort of-- I'm sometimes surprised, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you used to come home from school, who was there waiting for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after we lived with the Segals, as we spoke earlier, in the Russian family, we were then shunted from one place to another, because the Russian couple had a baby. And maybe they had pulled-- they wanted to get rid of all these eight extra people in this one room. So we were split up. My family-- in other words my mother, father, grandmother, and I-- were put with two women and a little boy in a room. And after that, we got our own hut for about eight months. My father was drafted. And, and a glass of water by my mother's bed froze overnight. When we woke up in the morning, her water was solid ice. And she said-- and I was very sick. I had this terrible bronchitis and whatever. It might have been pneumonia then, too. And we had made friends in Siberia. And their name was Josef and Rebecca Ostrov, O-S-T-R-O-V. And Josef [INAUDIBLE] Ostrov found us a place to live with a man whose family left to live with the woman's parents, who were very sick. So we lived there. But when I came home, most of the time, if we didn't live with another family, I came home to an empty hut. And I did the cooking, such as it was to be done, because my mother worked. And my grandmother no longer lived with us when we lived in that room with the man and-- whose wife had left. The room was divided with a, with a cloth. So he slept on one side. And my mother and I slept on the other. He was-- I came home to our hut by myself. And I was there alone until my mother came home from her job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the village all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was all Russian, very few Jews, just a few. maybe less than 100, maybe less than 50 Jews were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the attitude between the two groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know Jews. They didn't know who Jews were. Our first-- the first family we lived with-- Nina was her name, and Nikita-- I don't even know their last name-- asked my mother for a cross. And my mother said, we don't have a cross. And Nina said, I won't tell anybody. Just let me have your cross. And finally, my mother said, but we're Jewish. And Nina said-- I'll never forget it. This was the first house after the school and the mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother said, we're Jewish. And Nina said, how can you be Jewish? [INAUDIBLE], my mother's-- you know, my father's name, doesn't have a beard and a crooked nose. They didn't-- there was no anti-Semitism when we arrived. They didn't know Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later on, I'm sure there was. But again, I didn't feel it. This Josef Yurovich from St. Petersburg was a Jew. And his wife, Rebecca, was a pianist from Kharkov. They were Jewish. And they came with a immigration or transfer of Russian citizens from Leningrad and from Kharkov and from Moscow. And a large tractor factory opened on the outskirts of Rubtsovsk. And there was another school. And in that school, I studied with Anna Simnionimya, this literature teacher from the university in Moscow. And the algebra teacher, his name was Phillip. And I can't remember his patronymic. I just remember Phillip. I remember what he looked like. He made my life hell. But he tried. He tried to teach me, too. It's just that I couldn't learn-- excellent school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family learn about what was going on in the war? Were there newspapers or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were occasional newspapers. And there was a radio loudspeaker in the village. And then when the tractor factory moved to the outskirts, there were radio speakers there, too. And twice a day, news was broadcast. And we heard what was going on. And it was so difficult to believe. My father was in the army. And occasionally, letters from soldiers were read. And one morning, my mother woke me up and said, we better hurry up to go hear the radio. Because there will be a letter from, from Papa. And there was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the letter say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that he's fine and he sends-- there were, you know, like letters read on the radio, very brief, just to say, I'm alive. I'm all right, I hope you are all right. And-- but that she knew by ESP that the letter, it was very spooky to live with, with someone like that. But I'm like that, too, a lot. It's spooky for me to be like that, for myself. But news were-- traveled by the bush telegraphs or-- you know, it's an expression. One person tells another and another and another. And it finally reaches more and more people. But we did have the radio twice a day, and an occasional newspaper, but not that often. The newspapers were-- I, I can't remember reading a newspaper, actually. But there were newspapers. I'm sure of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the radio broadcast at the same time every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the morning and in the afternoon, I guess around 7 o'clock in the morning and 6 o'clock in the afternoon. When people-- before people went to work, they would stop and hear it. It was on loudspeakers. You could hear it outside, not in your house-- loudspeakers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go listen both times each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. That morning, we went. Because my mother said there would be a letter from my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes.
